entries.listed_title: Knowledge Systems Beat Prompting

article.plain_text:
I used to think the next productivity jump would come from better prompts.
A cleaner instruction. A stronger role. A longer context window. A sharper template.
That helped for a while. But after enough time working with AI agents, the same problem kept showing up in a different form: the model was not always the bottleneck. My context was.
I would ask an agent to help me write, plan, code, or decide something. Then I would realize I was feeding it a tiny slice of the real situation. The useful material was scattered elsewhere: a saved video lesson, a private note, a previous draft, a command output, a book idea, a project decision, a mistake I had already made once.
So the agent produced something polished, but not deeply useful.
Not because the model was stupid.
Because I gave it no map.
The wrong map: prompt engineering as the whole game
Prompting matters. Clear instructions still matter.
But prompt engineering becomes weak when it is treated as the entire operating system.
The wrong model says: if the output is shallow, the prompt must be bad.
Sometimes that is true. But often the deeper issue is this: the agent has no access to the relationships that make your work meaningful.
It does not know which ideas you have already tested. It does not know which examples belong to your audience. It does not know which sources are strong and which ones are just noise. It does not know the difference between a random saved link and a source that changed how you think.
So you keep adding instructions:
write in my voice
use my framework
connect it to my project
make it more practical
add depth
be less generic
But those instructions are trying to compensate for missing architecture.
A prompt is a request. A knowledge system is a memory with structure.
The better map: agents need a knowledge substrate
The useful shift is to stop thinking of AI as a prompt box and start thinking of it as an operator on top of a knowledge substrate.
That sounds abstract. The simple version is this:
Your saved material should not sit as dead storage. It should become a map.
A source should not only be saved. It should be extracted.
An idea should not only be highlighted. It should be connected.
A raw lesson should not only be archived. It should reveal claims, mechanisms, examples, contradictions, and next actions.
A project note should not only record what happened. It should tell the next agent what was decided, what failed, what worked, and what should not be repeated.
When that map exists, the agent can do more than respond to a prompt. It can retrieve, compare, connect, and act.
That is the difference between asking:
"Write me a post about AI productivity."
And asking:
"Use my notes on cloud learning, my last three content packages, the source brief from this video, my writing rules, and the open blockers in this project. Then draft a piece that connects knowledge systems to technical self-rebuilding."
Same model. Different ground.
The mechanism: capture, extract, tag, link, query, act
A useful knowledge system does not need to start as a huge graph.
It starts as a loop.
1. Capture the source.
Save the video, article, lab, conversation, book excerpt, command output, or project note.
2. Extract the useful claims.
What is actually being said? What changed? What mechanism is being shown? What mistake does this help prevent?
3. Tag the mechanisms.
Not only broad tags like "AI" or "productivity." Use functional tags: retrieval, context, workflow, agent handoff, quality control, Linux troubleshooting, career proof, writing system.
4. Link related ideas.
Connect this source to older notes, projects, drafts, mistakes, or frameworks. The value is often in the bridge, not the isolated note.
5. Create one next action.
A note without a next action is often just intellectual decoration. What should this source change in your work?
6. Let the agent query the map before creating.
Before drafting, coding, or planning, make the agent inspect the relevant notes instead of guessing from generic knowledge.
This is where output quality starts to change.
The agent is no longer improvising from a prompt. It is operating from a working memory.
My field note: this is where a local knowledge base becomes more than storage
This is why I keep my notes local, linked, and searchable.
Not because a note-taking app is magic. It is not.
But because a structured knowledge base gives AI agents a better starting point than a blank chat window.
When I leave my notes as scattered fragments, the agent behaves like a smart stranger. It can help, but it does not really know the terrain.
When I structure the work notes, draft goals, status, blockers, evidence, and links, the agent has something closer to a project memory.
It can see the difference between a public draft and private context. It can see the current state. It can see what is scheduled. It can see what was held. It can avoid repeating the same mistake.
That is not just productivity. That is quality control.
And in the AI age, quality control may matter more than raw generation.
Why this matters for technical self-rebuilders
If you are rebuilding your technical life -- learning cloud, Linux, AI, automation, writing, systems -- you are collecting more material than you can hold in your head.
Courses. Commands. Videos. Prompts. Errors. Notes. Articles. Job advice. Portfolio ideas. Tools. Frameworks.
At some point, the problem is no longer access to information.
The problem is returning to the right information at the right moment and turning it into proof.
A knowledge system helps you do that.
It turns learning into reusable context.
It turns mistakes into future instructions.
It turns saved sources into working material.
It turns scattered effort into visible progress.
And it gives your agents a way to support your actual path instead of producing generic advice for a generic person.
A practical first move
Do not try to build the perfect second brain this week.
Build one useful loop.
Pick one source you already saved but never used.
Then create a simple note with these headings:
What problem does this source solve?
What is the main claim?
What mechanism explains it?
What example makes it real?
What does this connect to in my current work?
What should I do next because of it?
What should an AI agent know before using this source?
That last question is the bridge.
You are not only writing notes for yourself anymore. You are writing notes for future collaboration between you and your tools.
Final reflection
The easy mistake is to chase the next prompt trick.
The harder, more useful move is to build the context layer that makes every future prompt smarter.
A better prompt can improve one answer.
A better knowledge system can improve the way you think, write, build, and delegate for months.
That is the shift I care about now.
Not AI as a magic box.
AI as an operator on top of a memory you deliberately built.

# Purpose  This vault is a source-grounded personal research system. 

Optimize for accurate retrieval, durable context, and traceable synthesis.

  # Directory rules  - /sources contains immutable imported material. 

- /wiki contains agent-maintained concept and entity pages. 
- /projects contains human-owned project notes. 
- /sessions contains compact handoff files. 
- Never modify files in /sources.
 - Never delete a file without explicit approval.  

# Evidence rules  - Preserve the source URL, author, publication date, and capture date.

 - Separate direct quotations from generated summaries. 
- Cite source files when making factual claims. 
- Mark unresolved contradictions instead of silently choosing one claim.  

# Editing rules  

- Prefer updating an existing concept page over creating a duplicate. 
- Show proposed structural changes before applying more than five edits. 
- Do not overwrite human-authored conclusions without approval.

entries.listed_title: We made 6 hires this year. All of them are AI. They share one brain.

article.plain_text:
Confession first: we run two startups, @NuvioHQ  and @octostudioco, and neither one has a marketing team.
What they have is six AI employees each. A blog writer. A social voice. An ads copywriter. A web copywriter. A cold outreach writer. A client conversation manager. Same six roles in both companies, and in each company they share one brain: every customer conversation, every competitor move, every lesson we've learned, in one place they all read from.
That last part is the whole story. Because the first brain we built made them dumber, and fixing it taught us more about AI than anything else we've done this year.
I want to walk through the full structure, including the version that failed, because the failure is the education.
 
The first agent
Version one looked like this:
references/ -> the settled canon: voice rules, customer pains, the proof bank, a checklist
brain/ ->  the learning stream, five subfolders: content-log, performance, customer-voice, competitor-intel, proof-candidates
skills/ -> five workers: blog, social, website copy, cold email
The thinking: references/ holds stable truth that every skill loads, brain/ collects new signals waiting to be promoted into the canon. Canon and learning. Clean separation.
The first agent also had no janitor. Nothing maintained the memory; signals piled up wherever a chat session happened to leave them. We noticed, and added two maintenance roles: an ingest skill to capture signals into the brain, and a consolidate skill to promote verified learnings into references with my sign-off.
That helped. It also delayed the real reckoning, because the problem wasn't missing maintenance. It was the architecture.
What the audit found
Weeks in, we ran a full audit of every file in the OctoStudio project. Nine duplications. Seven gaps. Four orphaned pages.
The specifics teach the lesson better than the totals. One client's retention story lived word for word in two places: inside a customer pains page and inside a proof-candidate page. The same two testimonials existed in both stores with different approval states, so the agent literally could not tell whether it was allowed to quote them. The rules governing the whole system were restated in five separate documents, which is a guarantee they drift apart. Produced content had no home at all. Deal records were squatting inside a "customer voice" page because no folder owned them. One page even described itself as "the living version of" another page, which is a duplication confessing in writing.
And the finding that stung most: the skills never actually read the brain. We had built a learning loop and left it open at the far end. The agent was collecting lessons it never used.
Underneath all of it sat one design flaw. The five-subfolder brain forced a classification decision at capture time. Every time something interesting happened, the agent had to decide which drawer it belonged in before saving it. That is exactly where misfiling happens, for AI agents and for humans. Ask anyone with seventeen Notion databases.
Why a brain can make an agent dumber
Before rebuilding, we read what's actually known about this problem: Karpathy's notes on LLMs needing their own kind of wiki, Anthropic's context engineering guidance, Chroma's research on context rot, Stanford's ACE paper on evolving agent context, and event-sourced memory patterns from software architecture.
They converge on one uncomfortable point: near-duplicate variants of the same fact are poison for retrieval. When an agent's memory holds three slightly different versions of one story, it doesn't get three chances to find the truth. It gets confused about which version is true. More memory, worse recall. That's how a brain makes an agent dumber.
The root cause in our design: references and brain were framed as siblings when they're really two layers of one knowledge system. Separate departments will always duplicate; layers don't have to.
My first redesign fixed the duplication but named the layers "canon vs evidence." Everybody pushed back, fairly: if the structure needs a glossary, humans won't maintain it. The final version uses words you'd explain to a new hire in one breath.
The structure that works
Both companies now run this, identically:
raw/ -> what happened
knowledge/ -> what we know
outputs/ -> what we made
skills/ -> how we work (the 6 employees)
Four folders. Here's why each one earns its place.
raw/ is the junk drawer, on purpose. Dated captures, append-only, no taxonomy at capture time. When a customer says something worth remembering, it gets saved with a date and a source line, and that's it. Filing happens later, when there's context, not in the moment. Append-only also means nothing is ever lost, only unprocessed.
knowledge/ is the wiki the agents maintain. Organized the way a human thinks: one page per client, one per competitor, one per pattern. Every page cites its raw sources and carries a confidence line, and when a page and raw disagree, raw wins and the page gets re-synced. One corner, we created a subfolder inside knowledge/ called playbook/, which is locked: voice rules, positioning, and the claims we're allowed to cite change only with my explicit sign-off. Facts about the world update freely; facts about who we are do not.
outputs/ is everything we make. Drafts carry a DRAFT prefix and a status line, shipped work drops the prefix and gets a row in a ledger. Six months from now I can answer "what did we publish in July and in which voice" from one file.
skills/ is the team. More on them in a second.
The governing contract shrank from five documents to six rules, stated once. And one addition I'd defend to the death: knowledge/log.md, an append-only line for every capture, every consolidation, every rule change, with sign-offs recorded. It sounds bureaucratic. It's the opposite: it's what makes delegation safe. I can leave for a week and read exactly what my team changed and why.
The old system had none of this. The comparison in one line: version one stored the same fact in multiple drawers and asked the agent to guess which was true; version two stores every fact once, links it to its evidence, and locks the parts that must not drift.
The six hires, by name
Each skill is a job description: what it's for, which knowledge it loads, how it works, and the quality bar it must clear. These are the six:
blog-content. Long-form education. Explains money and building to founders the way a calm senior colleague would. This article went through it.
social-content. LinkedIn, X, Instagram, in three distinct voices: mine (raw, build-in-public), my co-founder's (story-driven), and the brand's (educational). Same facts, three registers.
ad-copy. Paid variations for LinkedIn, Meta, Google. The only hire allowed to be repetitive on purpose.
website-copy. Landing pages and conversion sections. Words before pixels, always.
email-content. Cold first-touch to strangers, and only that. Short, honest, under 90 words.
client-conversations. The newest hire, and the one with the best origin story. It handles every message to someone we already know: replies, check-ins, support, review emails. It was created after the agent itself analyzed 30 days of our LinkedIn outreach, 322 conversations, and read every reply transcript instead of trusting the platform's sentiment labels. The labels said zero interested leads. The transcripts contained three booked calls and an actual paying customer. It found that our icebreakers were fine but our automated follow-ups were inventing statistics and burning goodwill, showed us the receipts, proposed its own job description from the evidence, and asked for sign-off. The sixth hire wrote its own role.
Two more roles work behind the team, and I don't count them as hires because they're the brain maintaining itself: context-ingest, the memory clerk that captures signals to raw the moment they appear, and consolidate, the wiki gardener that merges, re-syncs, flags stale pages, and proposes playbook changes for my sign-off.
How a skill actually works: the three-step loop
Every skill runs the same workflow: Goal, Create, Verify.
Goal: load the relevant knowledge pages (never write from memory), lock a short brief: audience, the one idea, the ask. If anything is unclear, ask one sharp question instead of guessing.
Create: write in the locked voice, take numbers only from the approved-claims section of the proof page, match the shape of real past winners stored as samples.
Verify: run every gate in a shared checklist before anything reaches me. Voice and banned words. Positioning and pain. Proof and honesty: any number must come from the approved list, framed as an average, or it doesn't ship. Focus. A human test: would a real founder stop and read this? And since last week, a reasoning gate: re-derive the most load-bearing claim through an independent path, label what's verified versus inferred versus guessed, attack your own conclusion before delivering. If a gate fails, the skill fixes and re-runs the whole checklist. It does not deliver with a note saying "this part still needs work."
That verify step is where most of the quality lives. A first draft from an LLM is fluent by default; fluent and correct are different properties, and only a checklist that runs every time tells them apart.
The loops, honestly
Two loops make the system compound.
The quality loop is tight: create, verify, fail, fix, re-verify, deliver. It runs inside every piece of work.
The learning loop is bigger and slower: work happens, signals get captured to raw, knowledge pages get re-synced, skills read those pages on their next run, outputs get ledgered, and results flow back into raw as new captures. Around it sits a rule I like: when the same shaped request shows up a third time, the agent proposes a new skill instead of improvising again. That's how roles get added to this team: by repetition, with evidence.
I want to be honest about these loops: they are not perfect loops. The agents run when we run them, so capture depends on someone doing the work in the project. A lesson can sit in raw for days before consolidate promotes it. The sign-off gates pause the learning loop on purpose, because I'd rather have a slow brain than a wrong one. Sometimes the agent forgets to capture a signal and it gets caught a session later.
They still work, for one structural reason: append-only means nothing is ever lost, only late. A leaky loop over a permanent record beats a perfect loop over a lossy one.
Six employees, one brain
Here's the framing that changed how I think about all of this.
Every company tries to build exactly this with humans. A team where everyone shares the same context, remembers every customer conversation, follows the playbook, checks their own work against the same standard, and writes down what they learned so the next person doesn't relearn it. We build wikis, onboarding docs, CRM hygiene rules, brand guidelines. And it decays, always, because human context lives in heads, and heads go home at six, forget, and eventually resign.
Six employees with one connected brain is the dream version of that, and the strange part of 2026 is that it's easier to build for AI employees than for human ones. Not because the AI is smarter, but because the AI forces you to write everything down properly, and then actually reads it.
The brain is the asset. The employees are replaceable; every model upgrade replaces them for free. What compounds is the structure: the wiki, the proof discipline, the samples of what worked with receipts, the log of every change. That's the part no one can take with them when they leave, because it never lived in anyone's head.
What I'd tell you to steal
Store every fact exactly once, and link to it. Duplication is how you rot your agent's memory, and two-store designs duplicate by architecture, not by accident.
Separate what happened from what you know. Append-only events in one place, curated understanding in another, and when they disagree, the events win.
Write job descriptions, not prompts. A prompt produces an answer. A skill with a goal phase, a creation standard, and a verification checklist produces a colleague.
Lock what must not drift, log everything that changes. The sign-off gate and the audit line cost minutes and buy the ability to delegate without anxiety.
And accept imperfect loops. Manual runs, human gates, late filing: all fine, as long as nothing is ever lost.
The question worth sitting with
If you had to write down how your company actually works, clearly enough that six very literal new employees could run with it tomorrow, could you?
That gap is the real project. The AI just makes it visible. 🤯

from uuid import uuid4
from opentelemetry import baggage, trace
from opentelemetry.context import attach, detach

@app.middleware("http")
async def propagate_conversation_id(request, call_next):
    conv_id = request.headers.get("X-Conversation-Id") or str(uuid4())
    ctx = baggage.set_baggage("conversation_id", conv_id)
    token = attach(ctx)
    try:
        tracer = trace.get_tracer(__name__)
        with tracer.start_as_current_span(
            "http.request",
            attributes={"conversation_id": conv_id, "route": request.url.path},
        ):
            response = await call_next(request)
            response.headers["X-Conversation-Id"] = conv_id
            return response
    finally:
        detach(token)
code.content:
```python
from uuid import uuid4
from opentelemetry import baggage, trace
from opentelemetry.context import attach, detach

@app.middleware("http")
async def propagate_conversation_id(request, call_next):
    conv_id = request.headers.get("X-Conversation-Id") or str(uuid4())
    ctx = baggage.set_baggage("conversation_id", conv_id)
    token = attach(ctx)
    try:
        tracer = trace.get_tracer(__name__)
        with tracer.start_as_current_span(
            "http.request",
            attributes={"conversation_id": conv_id, "route": request.url.path},
        ):
            response = await call_next(request)
            response.headers["X-Conversation-Id"] = conv_id
            return response
    finally:
        detach(token)
```

from agentcore.identity import requires_access_token

@requires_access_token(
    provider_name="enterprise-idp",         # IdP-backed OAuth provider
    scope="tool.retrieve_documents.invoke", # Per-tool scope, not user scope
    auth_flow="m2m",                        # Client credentials, not PKCE
)
async def retrieve_documents(query: str, k: int = 5) -> list[Document]:
    """
    Search the document corpus and return the top-k matches.

    AgentCore Identity has exchanged the agent's M2M credentials with the IdP for a JWT scoped to this tool's audience. The downstream tool validates `aud` and `scope` against the IdP's JWKS before serving.
    """
    return await tool_client.retrieve(query=query, k=k)

```python
from agentcore.identity import requires_access_token

@requires_access_token(
    provider_name="enterprise-idp",         # IdP-backed OAuth provider
    scope="tool.retrieve_documents.invoke", # Per-tool scope, not user scope
    auth_flow="m2m",                        # Client credentials, not PKCE
)
async def retrieve_documents(query: str, k: int = 5) -> list[Document]:
    """
    Search the document corpus and return the top-k matches.

    AgentCore Identity has exchanged the agent's M2M credentials with the IdP for a JWT scoped to this tool's audience. The downstream tool validates `aud` and `scope` against the IdP's JWKS before serving.
    """
    return await tool_client.retrieve(query=query, k=k)
```

article.plain_text:
Design decisions from shipping LangGraph agents on AgentCore Runtime with separated model and tool gateways, identity-scoped tools, and end-to-end OpenTelemetry observability.
TL;DR
Shipped a production agentic AI platform on AWS Bedrock AgentCore at a regulated investment-management firm, making four architectural decisions against AWS's canonical reference: (1) split the AI gateway in two — Enterprise AI Gateway for model traffic, AgentCore Gateway for tool traffic — since the flows have different auth, rate-limit, and audit needs; (2) scoped identity at the tool level via short-lived audience-pinned JWTs, so a prompt-injected LLM can't escalate to tools it wasn't pre-granted; (3) propagated one conversation_id via OpenTelemetry baggage through every hop, dual-routing to Langfuse (LLM view) and Datadog (SRE view) so an SRE can reconstruct any conversation from a single query; (4) applied PII redaction at the OTel Collector, not the source apps, keeping the compliance boundary in one auditable place. It's now the firm-wide reference architecture for future agent workloads — and if starting over, the biggest change would be building the eval harness in week one, not later.
Key Takeaways
A while ago, my team and I shipped a production agentic AI workload across one of the world's largest investment management firm's AWS estate. Regulated industry, real users, hard security and audit requirements, and a green-field platform under construction. By the time we finished the build, we'd discarded our original single-gateway architecture, revisited the identity model more than once, and learned why the canonical Bedrock AgentCore reference patterns don't quite hold up under enterprise constraints.
The pattern we ended up with — a dual-gateway perimeter that separates model traffic from tool traffic, identity scoping at the tool level via AgentCore Identity, and OpenTelemetry baggage propagation through every hop — is now the firm-wide reference architecture for every future agentic workload.
This article walks through the architecture, the four design decisions that mattered most, the things I'd do differently if I started over, and other patterns a production system needs. To make our pattern portable and let others see how the pieces fit together, I've distilled it into a public proof-of-concept (POC) reference implementation. Link at github.com/inetgas/agentcore-dual-gateway-poc.
Why the standard AWS reference architecture was not enough
The default AWS reference architecture for an agentic AI system looks roughly like this: a single AI gateway in front of a Bedrock-hosted model, agent code in a runtime (Lambda, ECS, or AgentCore Runtime), tools called directly from the agent code. The diagrams in AWS docs all draw it this way. It works for prototypes.
What it didn't account for in our context:
The auth model conflates model access and tool access. These are two completely different threat surfaces with two completely different control needs.
A single gateway becomes a chokepoint for two unrelated traffic shapes — long-lived streaming model responses and short synchronous tool calls. Different rate-limiting profiles, different failure modes.
Audit logs end up mixing two distinct concerns. Model-risk auditors (working against frameworks like SR 11-7) want prompt and response captured separately from tool-call audit trails.
Compliance teams wanted different policies on model traffic vs tool traffic — PII redaction on prompts, scope-token validation on tool calls.
User identity and agent identity are different things. The user signing in with SSO has authority to do X; the agent acting on the user's behalf needs authority to do one specific subset of X for this specific tool call. Collapsing them into one token would defeat least-privilege the moment the LLM took a wrong turn.
By the end of the design phase we had a different bar:
Models and tools must be governed independently, with different policy stacks and different audit streams
Each tool call must be authorized by a separate, narrowly-scoped token — not by the agent's session identity, and not by the user's full SSO token
Every operation must be traceable end-to-end via a single application-level identifier
The platform must support both REST tools and MCP servers without rewriting the agent
One enterprise IdP handles both user authentication and agent authorization, with the two flows kept structurally distinct
The architecture below is what came out of that constraint set.
The architecture
 
A user request enters at the chat UI, which generates a conversation_id and forwards the request with the ID in the headers. The orchestrator is a LangGraph ReAct agent running inside AgentCore Runtime — each conversation gets its own ephemeral microVM, isolated from every other conversation in flight.
The agent makes two kinds of outbound calls:
Model calls go through the enterprise AI gateway — a layer with PII redaction, prompt-safety policies, model routing, rate limiting, and centralized observability — configured as an OpenAI-compatible router fronting Bedrock and Azure OpenAI.
Tool calls go through AgentCore Gateway, which presents a unified registry of REST tools and MCP servers as a single tool-calling surface. The agent doesn't care whether retrieve_documents is implemented as a FastAPI service behind a load balancer or as an MCP server on the Gateway's MCP transport — the calling shape is identical.
Identity flows through the firm's enterprise IdP with AgentCore Identity acting as the agent-side broker. Two distinct OAuth 2.0 flows run in parallel, hitting the same authorization server but producing different kinds of tokens for different purposes:
Users authenticate via Authorization Code + PKCE. The chat UI redirects to the IdP, the user signs in (SSO + MFA), the UI exchanges the authorization code (plus the PKCE code_verifier) for an ID token and an access token. The user's identity rides with the conversation from that point on — every conversation_id is bound to a real human in the audit log.
Agents authenticate via M2M Client Credentials. When the agent needs to call a specific tool, AgentCore Identity exchanges the agent's pre-registered M2M client credentials with the IdP for a short-lived JWT scoped to that tool's audience. The agent never forwards the user's token to a tool. More on this in the next section.
Both flows resolve against the same authorization server, both produce JWTs validated against the same JWKS endpoint. One IdP, two distinct identity classes, one consistent audit trail.
State is split between short-term session events and long-term semantic memory, both held in AgentCore Memory with separate retention policies. Persistent knowledge corpora — the documents and reference data that tools like retrieve_documents search over — live behind their respective tools, on the other side of the Tool Gateway.
Observability is OpenTelemetry end-to-end. Every component emits OTLP to a shared OTel Collector, which applies PII redaction (more on this in Decision 4) and dual-routes: traces and LLM-specific events to Langfuse (prompt content, token counts, eval scores), traces and metrics to Datadog (latency, error rates, container health). conversation_id propagates as OTel baggage through every span across every service.
Design decision 1: Why two gateways, not one
The enterprise AI gateway handles model calls. AgentCore Gateway handles tool calls. Two gateways, two policy stacks, two audit streams.
What we considered instead: putting everything behind a single AI gateway — either a single AI gateway configured with separate route classes for models and tools, or an off-the-shelf single-pane gateway like LiteLLM extended with tool routing. One component, one deployment, one runbook. It's what most reference architectures show.
We rejected it for four concrete reasons:
Different rate-limiting profiles. Model traffic is tokens-per-minute over long-lived streaming responses. Tool traffic is requests-per-second over short synchronous calls. A single policy engine governing both ends up either too loose on one or too tight on the other.
Different identity primitives. Model calls authenticate as the caller plus a project quota. Tool calls require per-tool scope tokens — audience-pinned and narrowly scoped. The same gateway middleware can't elegantly enforce both.
Different audit requirements. Model-risk auditors want prompt and response captured against one control framework. Tool-use auditors want input arguments, output values, and caller identity captured against a different one. Putting both through one audit pipeline creates noise the compliance team would have to filter out — which they explicitly didn't want to do.
Different failure modes. A model-gateway outage is a degraded conversation. A tool-gateway outage is a different class of incident — the agent can still think, it just can't act. We wanted them to fail independently so on-call runbooks could differ.
What the trade-off costs: one more component to operate. We added a second deployment, a second set of alerts, a second runbook. The early operating period was noisier than I would have liked. After a while it was paying for itself in cleaner audit trails and independent blast radius.
Design decision 2: Identity scoping at the tool, not the orchestrator
This decision sits on top of the dual-flow IdP integration described in the architecture section. The user-side PKCE flow gives the agent a verified user identity (audit trail, group membership, organizational context). The tool-side M2M flow gives each individual tool call its own scoped JWT. The two never merge.
What we considered instead: the naive pattern that most LangGraph tutorials show. Agent authenticates once at session start; the user's access token (from PKCE) is forwarded down to every tool call; tools validate it and serve. One identity, propagated all the way down.
The problem with the naive pattern is the prompt-injection threat model. The user's access token represents everything that user is authorized to do across the platform. If the LLM is convinced — by adversarial input embedded in retrieved content, by indirect injection through tool outputs, by a user prompt the system can't perfectly sanitize — to call a tool the user technically has rights to but shouldn't be calling in this context, the tool happily executes. The LLM becomes a confused deputy operating with the user's full scope.
What we built instead: each tool function carries a @requires_access_token decorator naming the specific scope that tool needs (not the user's broad scope). When the agent decides to call that tool, the decorator triggers AgentCore Identity to exchange the agent's M2M client credentials with the IdP — never the user's PKCE token — for a fresh JWT with the tool's audience as the aud claim, the tool's invoke scope, and a TTL measured in seconds.
The end-to-end flow:
 
The code is small. The discipline is everything:
 
The tool validates the JWT against the IdP's JWKS endpoint — same issuer used for user authentication, same signature verification, same expiry checks, one cache. Even if the LLM is tricked into calling a tool it shouldn't, two things contain the blast radius:
AgentCore Identity won't request a token for a scope the agent wasn't pre-granted in the IdP. The agent's registered M2M scopes are the ceiling.
The tool won't accept a token whose audience doesn't match its own. Even if a token leaks from one tool's request to another's, it can't be replayed across the boundary.
For user-initiated tool calls — most of them in a chat system — the pattern refines further with OAuth 2.0 Token Exchange (RFC 8693). Instead of a pure M2M grant, the agent exchanges the user's access token plus its own credentials at the IdP for a new token whose act claim preserves both identities — the user as subject, the agent as actor. The tool sees, in the JWT alone: "this call was triggered by user X via agent Y, with scope tool.Z.invoke." No application-layer correlation needed; the audit chain is end-to-end cryptographic. RFC 8693 is the part of the OAuth 2.0 family most enterprise IdPs now support, and it's the cleanest answer to "who actually triggered this?" in agentic systems.
The user's identity is still preserved end-to-end — it rides in the OTel baggage and in the conversation_id binding — so audit can always answer "which human triggered this tool call." But the actual authorization for the call comes from a separate, narrowly-scoped, agent-derived token.
The auth path we landed on: treat the LLM as untrusted code path. Every output the LLM produces — including tool calls — passes through a privilege boundary it can't unilaterally cross. User identity flows in via PKCE for audit and context. Per-tool authority flows in via M2M for action. The two are never the same token.
What the trade-off costs: more moving parts at the identity layer, and the per-call latency of an Identity-to-IdP round trip on the first call of each tool (cached after that — the JWKS is cached at the tool, the M2M token itself is cached for its TTL). For us, both costs were worth it. For a system that doesn't process regulated data, the simpler session-token pattern could be fine.
Design decision 3: conversation_id as the join key across every signal
Every observability signal — every log line, every span, every metric label — carries a conversation_id that propagates as OpenTelemetry baggage from the chat UI through every downstream service.
What we considered instead: relying on OTel's built-in trace_id to stitch everything together. Trace IDs are the canonical answer — they're what OTel was built for. But trace IDs aren't application-meaningful. An on-call engineer staring at a Slack alert at 3am needs to pivot from "user X is complaining about conversation Y" to "show me everything about conversation Y across logs, traces, and metrics." conversation_id is the join key that maps to that mental model. Trace ID is the join key that maps to OTel's. They're complementary, not competing.
The implementation is a FastAPI middleware at every service entry point:
 
Every downstream call reads the baggage and includes it as a span attribute. Every structured log line includes it. The OTel Collector then dual-routes:
Langfuse receives LLM-specific events with conversation_id as the trace name. From a single Langfuse query I can see the full prompt, the full response, every tool call, token counts per node, and eval scores — for one conversation, in one view.
Datadog receives full distributed traces plus container metrics. From a Datadog query on the same conversation_id I can see latency at every hop, error spans, retries, and infrastructure events.
The same identifier in both. The same identifier in the application logs we ship to our central log store. A single value lets an SRE reconstruct the entire timeline of one conversation in under a minute — which has, more than once, been the difference between a clean incident retro and an unresolved customer complaint.
What the trade-off costs: discipline. Every team that writes a new service needs to remember to propagate the baggage, log structured events with the ID, and emit metrics with appropriate labels. We documented the decision in a platform-team runbook and built it into the service template. It's easy to forget early and expensive to retrofit late.
Design decision 4: PII redaction at the OTel Collector
PII redaction happens at the enterprise AI gateway for model traffic, and again — independently — at the OTel Collector for observability traffic. Two redactors, two rule sets, one consistent posture: nothing leaves your data plane raw unless you can prove it should.
This is one of three content-control layers in the architecture, each answering a different question about who gets to see what:
 
A redactor or policy good enough to satisfy one consumer is rarely the right shape for another. The gateway's redactor strips PII from outbound prompts but preserves the customer IDs the model needs to act. The Collector's redactor strips both, because the observability team has no business with either. AgentCore Memory's scoping does something different again — it controls what persists across turns and which principals can read it back, with retention rules that satisfy product UX and regulators simultaneously. Three different questions, three independent rule sets.
This decision is about the middle row — the observability path. The gateway's redactor is established as part of Decision 1's model boundary. AgentCore Memory's scope and retention are handled separately, through Memory's own access-control APIs and tenant-scoping primitives. The point of having three layers is that they evolve on their own clocks and serve their own audits; trying to consolidate them is the failure mode.
What we considered instead. Three options are possible for this layer:
Option A — Redact at the agent SDK. The agent's OTel SDK applies the redaction policy at emission time, using a shared internal library. Spans leave the agent already redacted; the Collector forwards them as-is. Clean from the Collector's perspective; high coordination cost across teams. Every agent runtime must import the redaction library, and the library must stay in lockstep across the gateway and every agent. A new PII rule that lands at the gateway but not at the agent SDK creates silent drift.
Option B — Redact at the OTel Collector. Spans flow in raw from the agent, the gateway, and the tools. The Collector runs a redaction processor before forwarding to Langfuse, Datadog, or any other backend. One redaction policy, version-controlled in one place, applied uniformly to all observability traffic.
Option C — Two-tier observability. Agent emits raw content to a regulated observability backend inside the data plane (self-hosted Langfuse in a private VPC, or a compliance-region instance); the gateway emits already-redacted content to a shared backend. Two backends, two trust boundaries, joined by conversation_id. Explicit story for "who saw raw content under what authority"; double the observability operations.
We chose Option B. Three reasons:
One redaction policy, one source of truth. The Collector becomes the single place where "what does PII mean for observability" is defined and version-controlled. Audit knows where to look. Engineers don't have to chase the same rule across the gateway, multiple agent runtimes, and a tools library.
The Collector is already security-critical. It's where OTLP authentication terminates, where sampling decisions happen, where exporters fan out. Adding redaction to its responsibilities is incremental, not new.
It composes correctly with the gateway's redactor without coupling to it. The gateway redacts so the model provider doesn't see raw PII. The Collector redacts so the observability backends don't either. Different threat models, different rule sets, independently evolvable.
The gateway and Collector redacting separately is the right answer specifically because they're protecting different consumers. Gateway-redacted content might still be too rich for Langfuse — account numbers and customer IDs that the model legitimately needs to act on, but that an observability team shouldn't have ambient access to. Collapsing the two into one redactor either leaks the rich content to the model provider or starves the observer of context the agent needed. They look at the same payload and answer different questions.
What the trade-off costs. The Collector becomes a security-critical component in a new way, and its failure modes need to be explicit. Three operational disciplines fall out of this:
Fail-closed semantics. A redaction processor that fails open is worse than no redaction — it gives you the false confidence of a redaction layer that isn't doing anything. The Collector pipeline must drop spans (or block forwarding) when redaction fails, not pass them through unredacted.
Redaction-rate monitoring. Sudden drops in redaction match counts are a leading indicator that either the rule library regressed or the upstream content shape changed. Alert on it.
Duplicate work in steady state. The gateway and the Collector both run regex/NER passes on similar content. CPU cost is real but small. The architectural clarity is worth it.
The decision composes with — but does not replace — the gateway's redactor or Memory's scope/retention controls. Three consumers (the model provider, the observability backend, the next conversation), three rule sets, three threat models. No shortcut.
What I'd do differently if I started over
Three things, in roughly the order I'd attack them:
1. Build the eval harness up front, not as an afterthought. I treated evals as a post-launch concern. By the time we needed them — to validate a new model version, to gate a release, etc — we didn't have the test set or the scoring infrastructure. If I started over, evals would be in the foundational scope, not the follow-up.
2. Service-discovery layer for tools from day one. We hard-coded MCP server endpoints in config. Every new tool meant a config change and a Pod restart. I wished we had a tiny service-discovery layer — agents discover available tools via a registry at session start instead of from baked-in config. A small upfront investment would have saved a lot of toil later.
3. Don't over-invest in gateway plugins early. We got fancy with AI gateway plugins early on — custom rate-limit logic, custom auth handlers, custom logging formats. Before long we'd thrown out a lot of it. The first gateway you stand up should be boringly simple. Add policies only when there's a real reason; the temptation to write gateway plugins because you can is one to actively resist.
None of these were catastrophic. All were preventable.
Other patterns a production system needs
This post is the case for 4 decisions I think enterprise AI platforms most often get wrong: the gateway split, per-tool identity scoping, conversation_id as universal join key, and PII redaction in OTel collector. A complete production agentic system needs more than these 4. Things I've worked on but haven't drilled into here — each worth its own post:
Cost guardrails and runaway-loop protection. An agent stuck in a tool-call loop can burn through tokens fast enough that finance notices before SRE does. Max-iterations per conversation, max-tokens per turn, circuit breakers on repeated tool-call patterns, per-conversation budget alarms — these aren't optional. The gateway enforces token rate limits at the network layer; the agent runtime needs to enforce iteration and conversation-level budgets, with hard cutoffs and clear failure modes the user sees.
Prompt-injection defense beyond the identity boundary. Per-tool scoping (Decision 2) contains the blast radius if the LLM is tricked into calling the wrong tool. It doesn't protect against the LLM being tricked into returning adversarial content to the user, exfiltrating context via tool arguments, or passing tainted input to downstream services that don't validate. Semantic guards on input, output classification, and tool-argument sanitization sit in front of and behind the identity layer. Defense in depth.
Eval harness and regression suite. Referenced in "what I'd do differently" — deserves its own post. A production agentic system needs a versioned test set, a scoring framework (LLM-as-judge, programmatic checks, or hybrid), and CI integration that blocks deploys on regression. Without it, you can't safely refresh models, refine prompts, or upgrade frameworks. Quality becomes whatever the last person to ship felt OK about.
Memory architecture, retention, and versioning. The diagram shows AgentCore Memory as one node, but that node hides several decisions. Short-term session memory vs. long-term semantic memory. Scoped memory for shared user/tenant context. Retention policies that satisfy both product UX and regulators. Embedding-model versioning for semantic memory — re-embedding stored memories when you swap embedding models is non-trivial work nobody plans for. The right memory architecture depends on the agent's task; the wrong one becomes the bottleneck before traffic does.
Streaming under the gateway. Long-running chat-completion responses (SSE) interact awkwardly with rate-limit accounting, trace assembly, and circuit breakers. Token-per-minute limits need to debit during the stream, not at completion. Spans need to record partial responses on client disconnect. Tool calls embedded in a streaming response break the simple request/response audit model. The patterns are well-known but they're not in the AWS docs.
Human-in-the-loop / approval gates. For high-risk tool calls — anything that moves money, modifies records of consequence, or sends external communication — the agent shouldn't just call the tool. It should propose the call, surface the arguments to a human, and proceed only on explicit approval. The orchestration pattern is straightforward; the UI integration is the actual work, and the failure modes (timeouts, declined approvals, partial completion) need explicit handling in the agent graph.
Cross-region disaster recovery for the AI gateway. Most enterprise platforms get DR-tested at the application layer. The AI gateway and its identity broker are usually the missing piece. Multi-region active-active for the model gateway, identity-broker failover, tool-gateway region affinity, and the cache invalidation problem when you fail over a JWKS cache — these are the moving parts. The day you need DR for your gateway is the wrong day to be designing it.
The public POC
The public POC reference implementation lives at github.com/inetgas/agentcore-dual-gateway-poc — a stripped-down runnable version of the pattern described above. Use it, fork it, tear it apart.
If you're working on similar problems at a regulated-industry shop or on an AI platform team, I'd love to compare notes. Reach out on LinkedIn.
Stanley W. Wang is an AI architect and practioner based in Atlanta. He has spent the last twenty years shipping cloud-native systems across financial services, retail, supply chain, and technology consulting. He is currently building production agentic systems. Find him on LinkedIn.

entries.listed_title: KARS (Kubernetes Agent Reference Stack) Makes Agents Look Like Real Infrastructure

article.plain_text:
@Microsoft introduced KARS, the Agent Reference Stack for Kubernetes, and I think it is worth taking seriously if you care about agents on the Microsoft stack.
I was able to see an early working version of KARS as it was being built (thanks to Pal), which makes the release even more interesting to me. It is good to see Microsoft putting real energy into self-hosted agents across KARS, Microsoft Scout, @OpenClaw, the Agent Governance Toolkit, and the other pieces forming around the stack.
The reason KARS matters is not complicated. If an agent is going to touch real business systems, it needs to run with the same kind of operational discipline we expect from any other production workload. A useful agent might read tickets, query a database, call a model, create a pull request, summarize customer history, check a safety form, or recommend an infrastructure change. That is not just a chat experience. That is an application identity with real implications.
Businesses already understand this problem when normal software is involved. We have service accounts, RBAC, network policy, audit logs, change control, CI/CD, Kubernetes, Entra ID, Defender, Purview, GitHub, Azure Policy, and a long list of boring habits that exist because production is not a playground. Agent demos often skip over those habits because the demo is easier when everything has broad access.
KARS is interesting because it starts from the access problem instead of treating it like a detail for later. The repo describes the intended posture with a short phrase that gets the point across: "zero credentials in the agent." The architecture docs describe a hardened sandbox where the agent does not hold the Azure credential, does not get direct network egress, and sends outbound calls through a Rust inference router sitting beside it in the pod.
 
In practical terms, the router becomes the control point for the things a business has to govern:
identity and token brokering
Foundry content-safety signals
token budgets and rate limits
tool policy and egress rules
MCP gateway calls
governance and tamper-evident audit
encrypted mesh and A2A traffic
sub-agent spawn and handoff
That is a lot of plumbing, but it is the kind of plumbing that lets a security team have a real conversation. If the agent is compromised, the failure should not automatically become a compromised cloud account, model backend, peer mesh, audit trail, or customer data path.
I also like that KARS is Kubernetes-native without pretending every business app needs to become a Kubernetes science project. The architecture docs call out Docker dev, local Kubernetes, and production AKS as different operating modes. The local Kubernetes path uses kind with the same general pod shape, UID separation, NetworkPolicy, and egress guard that production AKS uses, which helps avoid the common problem where the demo shape and the production shape are two different worlds.
The Microsoft stack angle is where this gets more interesting. OpenClaw gives builders and operators a runtime and command surface. KARS already has an OpenClaw runtime adapter, and the repo says OpenClaw to Hermes mesh is wired and tested end-to-end. Microsoft Foundry gives teams model access, content-safety signals, routing, and evaluation surfaces. Codex can turn repo work into issues, branches, diffs, checks, and PRs. Microsoft Scout points toward the always-on assistant pattern where agents sit closer to daily work. The Agent Governance Toolkit adds policy, audit, identity, trust, and SRE primitives that should be part of the stack instead of custom glue.
Put together, the story starts to look less like "AI wrote a thing" and more like "we have a governed runtime for useful work." That matters for a few practical business patterns:
Field operations agents can read job context, summarize site notes, check missing forms, and draft updates without roaming the whole network.
IT and SRE agents can diagnose alerts and propose remediation while approval, execution, and audit stay scoped.
Finance and procurement agents can help with invoice exceptions, vendor context, and approval prep without breaking separation-of-duty rules.
Support and account agents can summarize customer history and draft responses without crossing account boundaries.
Software delivery agents can work through OpenClaw, Codex, GitHub, and KARS so changes stay scoped, reviewed, tested, and traceable.
The caveat is important. KARS is a reference implementation, not an officially supported Microsoft product. The project-status section is clear about that, so I would not treat it like a drop-in enterprise agent platform without review.
The way I would evaluate builds upon my last article, it is boring on purpose. Pick one workflow that already has a business owner, a real source of truth, and a clear approval boundary. Run it locally, move it into the local Kubernetes shape, define the tool policy, define egress, define identity, set the budget, decide what requires human approval, and prove the audit trail before widening the scope.
Agents are becoming long-running, tool-using, network-reaching applications. If they are applications, they need application infrastructure. KARS brings that conversation back to familiar ground for Microsoft shops: AKS, Entra, Microsoft Foundry, @GitHub, policies, logs, approvals, and runbooks.
Start small, think big, and please do not give the bot admin because the demo was cool.
