entries.listed_title: Pinterest SEO 101: How It Really Works in 2026 (and What Changes with AI)

article.plain_text:
When most people in Italy hear “Pinterest,” they still think of it as the “pretty ideas app” — home décor, outfits, makeup, and light inspiration. In reality, brands and creators are increasingly treating it as a serious visual search engine, complete with SEO logic, sales funnels, and increasingly sophisticated AI integrations.While everyone is obsessed with Google’s generative search and ChatGPT, Pinterest is doing something quietly powerful: bringing conversational and agentic logic into a platform built on images, personal taste, and buying intent.In this article, I revisit the fundamentals of Pinterest SEO — profiles, boards, pins, and keywords — updated for today’s visual search and the platform’s AI experiments. The goal is to give you solid foundations so your content performs well with both people and the algorithms recommending it.

Is Pinterest a Search Engine?
Let’s be clear: Pinterest is not “just a social network.” It’s a visual search engine with its own dynamics. Every decision — title, description, image, board — sends signals to this system.
From moodboard to search engine
Recent studies (including Adobe’s research on 800 consumers and 200 businesses) show that a growing number of people use Pinterest as a starting point for ideas, products, and solutions — not just casual inspiration.It’s not another platform you scroll out of habit. You open it with a question in mind, even if that intent isn’t fully formed yet.
Typical searches include:
“Modern small living room ideas”
“Summer job interview outfits”
“Easy vegetarian dinner menu”

Unlike Google, the answer here starts with an image — not a blue link or an AI-generated summary.Visual search + intent-driven discovery
Pinterest has been working on “visual search” and “intent-driven discovery” for years. It doesn’t just match keywords; it tries to understand style, mood, aesthetic preferences, and shows you coherent ideas.Behind the scenes, this is powered by the Taste Graph — Pinterest’s proprietary map of user tastes built from images, text, saved pins, and boards.For content creators, this means you’re not just “posting pins.” You’re feeding a system that needs to understand:
What you do
Who you serve
What aesthetic and style you represent
Every decision — title, description, image, board — sends signals to this system.

How Pinterest’s Algorithm Actually Works
Behind the simple-looking visual feed is a sophisticated, real-time classification system. Understanding Pinterest SEO today means moving beyond the idea of a single linear algorithm. The platform now uses a multi-layered architecture.The classic pillars  Textual relevance: Keywords in strategic places (username, bio, board titles, pin titles & descriptions, alt text).  
Qualitative engagement: Saves (the modern “Repin”), clicks to destination URL, and time spent viewing the expanded pin.  
Freshness: New images or fresh creative variants get distribution priority. Duplicate identical files are penalized.  
Profile authority: Consistent, niche-focused accounts gain more algorithmic trust over time.
The multimodal layer (text + image)
Pinterest no longer treats text and images separately. Using Computer Vision, Object Recognition, and OCR, it analyzes both together to understand deeper meaning.  If your text says “minimal white living room” but the image shows a colorful maximalist space, the algorithm gets conflicting signals — and your visibility suffers.The generative layer (AI experiences)
The newest layer turns Pinterest into a conversational, assistive ecosystem. Features like Ask Pinterest, visual-first assistants, and dynamic optimization use the Taste Graph to deliver personalized, context-aware recommendations.Your content must now be clear and authoritative enough to be selected, understood, and recommended by intelligent agents.

What Pinterest SEO Really Means in 2026
Pinterest SEO is no longer about mechanical keyword stuffing. It’s about building a coherent ecosystem of signals (profile, boards, pins, visuals, and structured data) that proves your content’s authority to both the core algorithm and the AI models above it.
Three key requirements:  
Semantic alignment — Speak the user’s natural language and real needs.  
Multimodal consistency — Text and visuals must tell the exact same story.  
Clean, structured data — So generative AI can easily understand and use your content.

Where (and How) to Place Keywords on Pinterest
Profile name / Display name: Include your main vertical (e.g., “Debora Tesauro | SEO & AI for E-commerce”).  
Bio: Clear value proposition with 2-3 secondary keywords, written naturally.  
Board titles: Use real search queries (“Modern small living room ideas”).  
Board descriptions: Define the board’s purpose, audience, and content type.  
Pin titles: Strong keyword at the beginning + benefit hook.  
Pin descriptions: 2-3 full sentences + clear CTA.  
Alt text & file name: Clean, descriptive, and consistent.

Visuals Still Matter — A Lot
Pinterest is mobile-first and optimized for 2:3 vertical images. High clarity, good contrast, readable on-image text, and strong subject isolation are essential. The algorithm uses computer vision to understand what’s in your image — make its job easy.

How to Use AI for Your Pinterest SEO (the smart way)
Use AI as a multiplier, not a replacement:  For keyword research and clustering  
For generating title/description variations  
For predictive analysis of visuals
But always keep human oversight for strategy, tone, and final quality. No keyword stuffing. No generic AI slop.

What Changes with Generative Search (and What Stays the Same)
What hasn’t changed:
The core of SEO remains the same — understand the user, deliver real value, and structure information clearly. Whether the final output is a traditional web page, a Pin grid, or an AI-generated response, the fundamentals stay human-first.
What’s new:
Discovery is becoming more conversational and multimodal. People ask complex, natural-language questions, and AI combines multiple signals (saved pins, product catalogs, aesthetics, context) to deliver richer answers.Your content needs to be clear enough for both humans and AI models to understand and combine it effectively.

entries.listed_title: How to earn $$$ with clips (and unlock rev share faster)

article.plain_text:
Most people fail trying to unlock X's creator program. I unlocked rev share on my first try. Two weeks later I earned $1,546.84. I'm going to help you unlock rev share & start earning. Bookmark this article for later.
1. Proof
 
I did not have lots of followers when I started. I still don't have a large following. I was able to unlock rev share and start earning cash by posting clips everyday.
 
To unlock rev share, you need to
Subscribe to Premium
Have 500 verified followers
and get 5M impressions within the last 3 months.
 
The last two are hard. But not impossible. In fact, it's easier than you think. You need to post content others want. The reason most people fail at unlocking rev share is they post things only they and only a handful of people care about. I used to do this too. The easier path is to give people what they already crave. Clips.
2. Post clips to unlock rev share faster
Unlocking rev share is an impressions game. You need content that can reach people who do not follow you. That is why clips work so well. A good clip can escape your follower graph and get tested in the For You feed.
If X finds the right audience for it, the clip can keep moving without you doing anything else. A clip can get discovered by people who have never seen you before. That matters a lot when you are trying to qualify for monetization.
This is how a small account can get big-account reach. You do not need 100K followers to unlock rev share and earn.
 
The clip that made me take X rev share seriously was a clip about retardmaxxing. I had posted it when retardmaxxing was a trending topic.
It got 1.1M views. Before that, I was posting clips once in a while. After the clip hit, I realized this was actually doable. One clip had generated a huge chunk of the impressions needed to unlock rev share. 
That started the 3-month push. Not because I thought every clip would do 1M views. Most won’t. But it proved that clips could help me unlock creator rev share if I posted consistently.
The hard part is doing it consistently. Most people post one clip, hope it goes viral, and stop when it doesn’t go anywhere. You need to realize this is a volume game. Most clips will flop. But you just need a few to hit it big.
You need to find enough good clips and post them. And you need a system to do it again and again without missing a single day.
3. The algo loves clips
X is pushing clips harder than ever. There is a huge appetite for short clips on X right now. This makes sense. I’ve noticed that the algorithmic push can be stronger than reposts from large accounts. That is important for small accounts.
 
Clips keep people on X for longer. They are easy to watch, easy to reply to, and easy for X to test. X behave more like a TikTok-style recommendation system now. It tests a clip with a smaller audience first. Then watches the early signals.
If the clip gets attention with replies, watch time, bookmarks, or reposts, X expands the audience. When X decides to push a clip, it can aggressively push it.
I do not claim to know exactly how X’s Grok-based recommendation system works. But I know what I keep seeing. Smaller accounts are getting clips pushed hard. That tells you something. There is a lot of demand for clips, and X is aggressively pushing them to people's For You page.
4. Target large audiences
If your goal is impressions, you want to post clips that can go viral. That means a lot of people have to find it interesting. Or in other words, pick videos sources to create clips from that already have a large audience. 
 
The best videos to clip usually have one or more of these things:
Novelty - New ideas/topics that X hasn't seen before
Famous people - Celebrities, politicians, podcasters etc.
Trending topics - Stuff in the news/breaking
Debate-heavy subjects - Stuff people will argue/disagree about
A clip does not need all those. But it needs some reason for people outside your audience to care and engage.
5. Pick a niche for your account
I recommend targeting your account towards a specific niche. The niche you choose could be in AI, technology, sports, entertainment, hollywood, politics, podcasts etc.
Pick the niche that interests you the most. It can even be fan account.
 
Picking a niche helps X to categorize you account correctly. This allows it topush your clips to the relevant audience who already love your type of clips. Picking a niche will maximize account-audience fit so you can maximize your impressions.
Including your niche in your bio helps you get to 500 verified followers faster. Most people on X do not pay for Premium, so getting 500 verified followers can take a while. I was able to get to 5M impressions in 2 months, but getting 500 verified followers took longer. I noticed that as soon as I added info about my niche in my bio that the viewers of my clips cared about (ex. being a clips account), I started to get more verified followers at a faster rate. If I had added it earlier, i wouldn't have had to wait as long to get monetized.
So pick a niche and post clips. Do it everyday.
6. Strategy: Post the next-best clip
This is one of the biggest things I learned. The obvious clip is not always the best clip for you to post. When a new podcast or interview drops, the large clipping accounts in your niche will all race to post the most obvious clips first. 
 
Then the aggregators will post it/quote post it. At that point, the demand for that clip have been met and duplicate versions are harder to push.
So instead of trying to compete with the large accounts, I like finding the next-best clips. These are moments the big accounts miss, ignore, or do not have room to post. This is where smaller accounts can win.
I use FindaClip's rankings to help me find the next-best clips. It provides options and helps me find clips other people missed so that I don't have to compete with them for impressions.
7. It's hard to predict winners
This is why volume matters.
I have posted clips I was sure would go viral and they did nothing. I have also posted clips just to have something up, and those took off. That happens more than people think. The algorithm makes the final call. And your job is to give it options.
 
I like posting 3-4 good clips per day and watching what X starts to push. One clip might die. One might get a small test push. One might suddenly start moving. That is the clip you want to pay attention to.
Posting 3-4 good clips per day is possible. Doing it every day is where most people give up. FindaClip helped me keep the pipeline full without missing days.
I also started using X's audience active-times chart more.
 
My current approach is to post clips 3-5 hours before the most engaged windows. That gives X time to test the clips before the peak traffic window. Then if one starts to get signal, it has a better chance of getting pushed harder when more people are active.
8. My system for unlocking rev share
 
My biggest mistakes were:
I did not post enough. I was not consistent enough. I posted the same obvious clips everyone else was posting. I mixed in too much non-clip content and talked about random stuff no one cares about. And I assumed I knew which clip would go viral. I used to do this manually.
Manual clipping works, but it is hard to sustain. It can take several hours just to get 3-4 good clips. You have to watch long videos, find the moments, cut them, write the post, and do it again the next day.
Doing that once is easy. Doing that every day for 3 months is too hard for anyone. Most people give up.
That is why I built FindaClip.com.
I wanted a system that could help me find lots of good clip quickly so I could post several clips everyday.
FindaClip made unlocking rev-share possible for me because it solved the issues of
speed
finding the best (and next best) clips before others
consistency, and volume.
It's not about finding the one magic clip. The edge comes from having good clips to post everyday than the person doing everything manually. You can unlock rev share without FindaClip. But it is a lot harder.
If you are serious about using clips to grow on X and getting paid, having a system matters.
This is the system:
Post 3-4 good clips per day. Everyday.
Pick a niche so X can categorize your account.
Avoid chasing the same obvious clips as everyone else.
Post before your audience's active windows.
Let the winners run.
That is how clips helped me unlock monetization and start earning X rev share.
Bookmark this article so you don't lose it.
Have questions? Leave a comment.

entries.listed_title: The AI Anime Blueprint: From Concept to Production Pipeline (Tutorial & Prompts Included)

code.code:
Animate the provided first frame into a 15-second cinematic anime football epic trailer in 16:9 widescreen. Keep the same rain-soaked World Cup-style stadium, same wet pitch, same dramatic floodlights, same two main characters, same emotional high-stakes atmosphere, but render everything in a high-budget anime sports film style rather than live action.
STYLE:
Cinematic anime sports drama, high-budget anime movie trailer, detailed cel shading, painterly stadium backgrounds, dramatic shonen sports intensity, hand-drawn rain effects, stylized water splashes, glowing floodlights, subtle film grain, expressive close-ups, slow-motion action, anime impact frames, speed lines, emotional visual pacing. The video should feel bigger than football, like a final battle inside a stadium.
CHARACTER LOCK:
Arman is always the crimson-kit striker and captain. He has short wet black hair, olive skin, intense dark anime eyes, light stubble, deep crimson football kit with black trim, no real logos, captain armband, black socks, worn white boots.
Dante is always the charcoal-kit goalkeeper. He is tall, pale, shaved head, sharp blue anime eyes, dark charcoal goalkeeper kit, padded gloves, black boots.
Do not swap their roles, outfits, faces, or positions. No real footballers, no real team branding.
TIMELINE:
0–2s:
Start from the first frame. Low grass-level anime shot near the wet ball and penalty spot. Arman stands over the ball in heavy rain. Dante is visible far away on the goal line. Floodlights glow through mist with strong anime bloom.
2–3.5s:
Cut to Arman close-up. Rain runs down his face in stylized droplets. His dark eyes narrow with determination. His wet black hair clings to his forehead. The crowd behind him becomes a painterly blur of color and light.
3.5–5s:
Cut to Dante close-up. He flexes his gloves and lowers into a ready stance. His sharp blue eyes reflect the ball. Blue-white floodlight outlines his body like an anime rival aura.
5–6.5s:
Low shot of Arman’s boots stepping backward from the ball. Mud, water, and grass blades shift under his feet with exaggerated anime detail. The ball remains still on the penalty spot.
6.5–8s:
Wide side-profile shot. Arman and Dante face each other across the rain-soaked pitch like duelists before battle. The stadium feels enormous, with flags waving, mist drifting, and floodlights cutting through the rain.
8–10s:
Arman begins his run-up in slow motion. Camera tracks low beside his legs. Water sprays from the grass with each step. Add subtle crimson motion streaks from his kit and captain armband.
10–11.5s:
Impact moment. Arman’s boot strikes the ball. Use a stylized anime impact frame: sharp black-and-white speed lines for a fraction of a second, then a burst of water droplets, grass fragments, and glowing motion trails.
11.5–13s:
The ball flies through rain toward the goal in slow motion. Camera follows from behind and slightly below the ball. The stadium lights stretch into anime speed lines, and droplets spin away from the ball.
13–14s:
Dante dives fully across the goal, gloves outstretched, body suspended in rain and light. His charcoal kit is rim-lit with blue-white glow. The ball passes near his fingertips.
14–15s:
Final dramatic anime hero shot. The net ripples in the background as the stadium erupts. Arman stands in the rain, chest rising, head slightly lifted, floodlights blazing behind him. End on an emotional anime sports-film frame, with rain, light, and crowd color surrounding him.

MOTION:

Hard white anime stadium floodlights, misty halos, wet grass reflections, cool blue shadows, warm orange skin highlights, dramatic cel-shaded contrast, cinematic lens flare.
ENDING:
End with a powerful emotional anime frame that feels bigger than football: Arman alone in the rain, stadium roaring behind him, the net moving in the distance, destiny decided.

article.plain_text:
AI anime storytelling used to feel like a lucky accident.
You wrote a beautiful prompt, asked for a cinematic scene, and hoped the model would understand the character, the world, the emotional beat, the shot language, and the ending all at once. Sometimes it worked. Often, the face changed, the outfit drifted, the camera missed the moment, or the story looked impressive but felt unfinished.
That was acceptable when creators were only testing what AI video could do.
But now creators are building anime shorts, serialized character pages, visual novels, music videos, lore channels, product mascots, and social-native story worlds. For that kind of work, a single lucky generation is not enough.
Anime storytelling needs character identity.
It needs mood.
It needs continuity.
It needs timing.
And most importantly, it needs a workflow that can be repeated.
That is what APOB AI is built for.
APOB AI is an independent AI influencer platform designed for creators who want more than isolated generations. With a workflow that brings together the AI Influencer Generator, GPT Image 2.0 inside Chat to Generate and Chat to Edit, and Seedance 2.0 inside Image to Video Ultra S, creators can move from loose prompting to a structured production system.
Instead of asking one video model to invent the cast, the anime style, the scene, the performance, and the final movement in one pass, creators can build the character first, turn that identity into a storyboard, edit the keyframes for story clarity, and then guide Seedance 2.0 with visual references and time-coded direction.
The future of AI anime is not just better prompting. It is better pre-production.
From Anime Idea to Repeatable Story System
For this workflow, imagine creating a short anime story called The Girl Who Saved the Last Starlight. The setup is simple: a quiet teenage girl finds a fading fragment of blue starlight in an empty train station after midnight. She follows it through a rain-soaked city and learns that every small act of courage makes the light brighter.
The old workflow was:
prompt -> anime clip
The stronger workflow looks like this:
AI character model -> anime character sheet -> visual story bible -> storyboard panels -> Chat to Edit continuity pass -> Seedance 2.0 video prompt -> captions, voice, sound, and iteration
That shift matters because it turns AI anime from a one-shot experiment into a controlled creative pipeline. The creator is no longer waiting for the model to guess the story. The creator is giving the model a production package.
Step 1: Build the Anime Lead With the AI Influencer Generator
A memorable anime short starts with a recognizable lead character. Not just a girl with blue hair. Not just a generic anime protagonist. Not just a style tag that says cinematic anime and hopes the model fills in the rest.
The audience should recognize the same face, silhouette, hairstyle, costume, expression range, and emotional energy from the first frame to the final shot. This is why the workflow begins with the AI Influencer Generator.
The goal is to create an original anime character model that can carry multiple scenes, not a single image that disappears after one post.
 
This character model becomes the identity base. Every image after this point can begin from the same cast instead of asking a new prompt to rebuild the protagonist from memory.
Step 2: Create a Production Character Sheet in Chat to Generate
Once the anime lead is defined, the next move is to create a character sheet. This is where GPT Image 2.0 inside Chat to Generate becomes useful. The goal is not only to make a beautiful image. The goal is to create a reference that can guide every later frame.
For anime storytelling, a character sheet locks the face, hair, outfit, color palette, posture, expression range, and silhouette before the video begins. If the sheet is weak, the video model has to guess. If the sheet is clear, the whole production has an anchor.
Chat to Generate Prompt: Anime Character Sheet
 
 
 
This is the first major unlock. The creator is no longer asking for a random anime girl. They are working with a specific character who can survive multiple shots.
Step 3: Generate the Story Bible Image
A good anime short needs more than a protagonist. It needs a world. Before making a storyboard, create one visual story bible image that captures the environment, lighting, emotional tone, and symbolic object. For this example, the symbolic object is the fading blue starlight.
Chat to Generate Prompt: Anime Story Bible
 
 
 
This image becomes the emotional north star. It tells every later generation what the film should feel like, not only what it should contain.
Step 4: Build a 12-Panel Anime Storyboard
Now the creator can turn the idea into a sequence. This is where Chat to Generate can produce storyboard panels with the same lead character and a clear beginning, escalation, and payoff.
Chat to Generate Prompt: 12-Panel Anime Storyboard
 
 
 
Step 5: Turn the Key Storyboard Into Video With Seedance 2.0
Once the character sheet and storyboard are ready, the final move is Image to Video Ultra S with Seedance 2.0. The video prompt should not merely say make this cinematic. It should tell the model how the scene moves over time.
For the strongest result, use the storyboard or a chosen keyframe as the visual reference, then write a time-coded movement prompt that controls camera, action, lighting, expression, and emotional pacing.
Seedance 2.0 Prompt: Cinematic Anime Video Scene
 
 

Step 6: Iterate Like a Director, Not a Gambler
The first Seedance 2.0 output can already be strong, but the workflow becomes more powerful when the creator reviews it like a director. Do not only ask whether the clip looks good. Ask whether the story reads.
Does Airi look like the same character from the character sheet?
Does the starlight remain visually consistent?
Does the emotional shift from lonely to hopeful happen clearly?
Does the camera support the story beat instead of distracting from it?
Would this clip work as episode one of a repeatable anime series?
If the answer is no, the creator does not need to restart from a blank prompt. They can return to Chat to Edit, adjust the storyboard, strengthen one keyframe, or rewrite only the time-coded section that failed.
Why This Workflow Changes AI Anime Creation
The important part is not that one tool makes a beautiful anime image or one model makes a cool video. The important part is that the tools work together as a production system.
With APOB AI's AI Influencer Generator, GPT Image 2.0 inside Chat to Generate and Chat to Edit, and Seedance 2.0 inside Image to Video Ultra S, creators can move from unpredictable outputs to a full production workflow:
The AI Influencer Generator gives the story a reusable character identity.
GPT Image 2.0 inside Chat to Generate turns that identity into character sheets, story bibles, keyframes, and storyboard panels.
GPT Image 2.0 inside Chat to Edit makes continuity and story revisions possible without losing the whole direction.
Seedance 2.0 inside Image to Video Ultra S turns the prepared visual references into cinematic movement.
That is the difference between loose prompting and structured production. Loose prompting asks the model to surprise you. Structured production gives the model a clear cast, a visual language, a sequence, and a performance target.
For creators, this means AI anime storytelling can finally become repeatable. One short can become a series. One character can become a channel. One emotional scene can become a world.
And the best part is that the workflow still feels creative. The creator is not replaced by the model. The creator becomes the showrunner: designing the character, shaping the world, revising the storyboard, directing the camera, and deciding when the story finally works.
The New Rule
Do not prompt the whole anime short at once.
Build the character.
Build the world.
Build the storyboard.
Edit the continuity.
Then animate with direction.
That is how creators move from random AI anime clips to a real storytelling workflow.
Try it now: https://mega.apob.ai/article
🎁Quote or retweet this post to get 5,000 credits to try it out — plus access to more dedicated prompts and workflows.
Disclaimer: APOB AI does not support or permit the creation of deepfakes. Users are responsible for intellectual property rights and legal compliance for their generations. Please review APOB AI's Terms of Service for full details.

article.plain_text:
UGC that looks like a real creator filmed it - with no creator.
No casting, no shipping samples, no week of reshoots. Drop in a product photo, describe the spot, and walk out with an influencer-style video, voice-over and all.
 
Make your first UGC ad in one chat - or one line.
Drop in a photo of your own product and walk away with a finished influencer video. Run it as a guided conversation, or fire it off from your terminal.
Open Sogni Chat
Get the Sogni Skill
What's happening behind the scenes:
Image model Builds the cinematic storyboard from your photo.
Video model Animates the sheet into the finished spot, with audio.
Spark credits Meter each render - you approve the cost before it runs.
The idea
UGC without the creators.
User-generated content used to mean people - finding creators, negotiating rates, shipping product and waiting for footage. Sogni collapses the whole thing into one move: a product photo in, finished influencer videos out.
For a small brand the bottleneck was never the idea - it was the production. A product photo is easy. A week of UGC from real creators is not. Sogni puts that whole chain into a single conversation - or a single command.
You drop in a reference image - a fragrance, a shampoo, a serum - or just describe it. The agent treats the product as the anchor and generates the creator and the scene around it. Then Seedance 2.0 animates it into a finished social video, native audio included. One detail worth knowing up front: Seedance won't clone a real person's face, so every creator is generated and unique - UGC energy with none of the likeness headaches.
There are two ways to drive it: Open Sogni Chat ↗ for a guided conversation in your browser, or Get the Sogni Skill ↗ to run the same engine inside your own agent - Claude Code, Codex or Hermes - handy when you want to fire off ten campaign stills in one batch.
One workflow, any product
Reference photo → AI creator → video.
Every time.
The same flow works for any category: start from a real product photo, let Sogni build the creator and the scene, then render with Seedance 2.0. The whole workflow is in the examples below.
Step 01 · The start image
An imperfect photo of your product - that's the only input you need.
Start with what you already have. Here it's one snapshot of the EDGE bottle in hand - black glass, the wordmark on the front. No studio, no lightbox.
From this moment the image is the product anchor: it keeps the bottle's shape, glass, label and logo placement consistent across every still and every video the agent makes next.
It works for any category, from a fragrance to a gadget to a garment, and the photo doesn't even need to be perfect. The agent only needs to recognise the product, not copy your lighting, so a quick phone shot is plenty.
No product yet? You can describe it instead - the agent can invent the bottle, the brand and the world from words alone. A reference photo just makes brand consistency effortless.
 
Step 02 · Install the Skill in your agent
Put Sogni inside Claude Code, Codex CLI or Hermes.
The Sogni Creative Agent Skill installs in one command - it adds the CLI and the skill and prompts for your API key (grab it from dashboard.sogni.ai). Pick your runtime on the right and copy the command.
The fastest path auto-detects Claude Code, Codex CLI and Hermes in one go. Prefer no install at all? Everything here also runs in chat.sogni.ai - just a browser tab.
All install options live at sogni.ai/agent.
With the skill loaded, drop in your product photo and describe the spot in plain language - the agent plans it, estimates the cost in Spark, and routes the job to Seedance 2.0. We'll do exactly that next.
 
Step 03 · Ten shots at once
One batch command. A full still campaign.
This is where the Skill earns its keep. Ask for a batch and it generates 10 stills at a time - a different creator and location in each, every aspect ratio you'll need, plus clean product-only shots. Same bottle, same label, every frame.
 
Step 04 · The influencer cuts
Now the video. Seedance does the acting.
Same skill, now for motion. Hand it the bottle photo and a UGC brief - who's holding it, where, which aspect ratio - and Seedance 2.0 generates the creator and the movement, native audio and all.
You don't have to over-specify. Name the creator, the setting and the format, and the agent fills in the rest: pacing, framing, lighting, even the voice-over script. Want a different take? Ask for a new creator, a calmer delivery or a tighter crop, and only that part changes while the bottle stays locked frame to frame. Prefer a different engine? Swap Seedance 2.0 for LTX 2.3 in the same sentence.
Terminal - install the skill first: npx setup-sogni-agent-skill
 
 
From here it's all variations. Keep the same creator and drop them into a fresh scene, or spin up a brand-new one - a different guy, or a female creator entirely. You choose how they sound, too: a voice-over narrating over the shot, or the creator talking straight to camera. Three cuts here, each a different person, the bottle byte-for-byte the same.
 
Finish the set with a clean product film - the same bottle, no creator, just the hero shot for the top of the feed or an end card. One photo has now produced a campaign of stills and a stack of videos.
 
Step 05 · Way 1 · the storyboard route · chat.sogni.ai
Two ways to make this ad. We start in the chat.
There are two ways to build the LUMI spot - and they share one trick. Seedance 2.0 won't take a real person's face, so instead of fighting it you anchor your real product and let the model generate the creator around it. We start in chat.sogni.ai with the shampoo bottle as the reference.
Strengthen the prompt while you're at it: spell out that the logo, the captions at the bottom and any other details must stay identical across the whole video, so nothing drifts between shots.
Why this matters. The product is what your audience recognises - so that's what we lock. The face is generated fresh, which keeps you clear of likeness and release-form issues entirely.
 
1 · The brief. Type the spot in plain language and attach the storyboard and the product reference. Set the format, the mood, and a firm product-consistency note. New to storyboards? Our guide Storyboards that move walks through making one from scratch.
 
2 · The plan. The agent inspects the board and the product, works out the beats, and gets the render approved - all before it draws a frame.
 
3 · Delivered. The finished video lands right back in the conversation - with Extend, Refine and Generate-audio buttons to keep shaping it without ever leaving chat.
 
Here's the storyboard it drew along the way: nine panels with timecodes and voice-over baked in - bottle on the shelf, the shower ritual, the blow-dry payoff, the hero packshot. That's the beauty of a storyboard: you get a preview of every scene, and you can define and re-tweak it as many times as you like before spending a credit on the video.
 
Tip: drop in a styling tool like this dryer for extra realism.
And the finished spot: a real-looking influencer with natural skin texture and wet hair, the LUMI bottle never changing - a polished 16:9 beauty ad straight out of the chat. It even picks up the hairdryer we put in the storyboard - dropping in a styling tool like that is a nice touch for extra realism.
 
Step 06 · Way 2 · the one-line route · the terminal
No storyboard. No detailed prompt. Just the bottle and a sentence.
The second way skips the storyboard entirely. Drop only the bottle into the Sogni Skill in your terminal and ask for a UGC ad in one line. The agent writes its own brief, picks Seedance, plans the beats and renders - start to finished MP4, hands-off.
Terminal - install the skill first: npx setup-sogni-agent-skill
Sogni Agent Skill 
 
That's the entire workflow - and it's the one you'll reuse forever. Open the Sogni Skill in your terminal, swap in your own product as the reference image, and type one sentence describing what should happen. No storyboard, no prompt engineering - the agent fills in the rest: it drafts the brief, picks the model, estimates the Spark cost, renders, and saves the finished MP4.
New product next week? Drop its photo in as the reference and send the same one-liner - a fresh ad in minutes. Want a vertical cut for Reels, a square for the feed, or a different scene? Change just that part of the sentence and run it again; everything else stays locked.
Worth knowing - the honest bit. The first render got flagged: a wet-hair bathroom scene tripped Seedance's content policy - a common false positive for anything shower-adjacent. The agent simply retried with a vanity setup and it went through clean. When a beauty render bounces, nudge the scene (vanity, sink, getting-ready) rather than the wording, and it usually clears.
 
Try it now
One photo. A whole content calendar.
That's the point of Sogni. The same photo that anchors your product writes the brief, casts the creator and renders the video - metered, reversible, and locked to your brand the whole way. No casting calls, no shipping samples, no waiting on footage.
Start in the chat or run it from your terminal. Then save the chain as a Workflow Template and the next product drop is one prompt away - a fresh batch of stills and a stack of videos, not a pile of separate calls.

Open Sogni Chat
Get the Sogni Agent Skill

Full version of the article with prompts: https://blog.sogni.ai/blogs/ugc-without-creators/
 
Thank you for reading! ♥️

entries.listed_title: My semi-complicated relationship with social media

article.plain_text:
I've been making videos on and off since 2018, and I wanted to write some musings on what I've learned in those last 8 years of content creation, as well as how my relationship with social media has changed over all that time.
What drew me to content creation
Let's start before 2018. I was a relatively lonely kid in both high school and college, and I turned to TV often for comfort. A common night for me would be settling in with a Chipotle burrito or yaki udon and watching hours of content.
I lived in the NigaHiga, KevJumba, WongFu Productions, David So, Jenna Marbles side of YouTube. WongFu was the master of beautifully produced storytelling. David So was amazing at comedy and making me laugh (but also weirdly inspiring) through pure crassness. And others showed me that you could gain massive followings by just entertaining the most weird and crazy things on the internet, as long as you were willing to film and edit it together.
Towards the end of my college career, I became severely depressed. One facet of my depression came from the feeling that I had no identity of my own. I would often google "50 things to do when you don't have anything to do." Meanwhile, my peers were grinding towards diamond in League, on a college sports team, or excelling at schoolwork, and generally had time for healthy friendships and relationships. Watching content got me through a lot of that difficult time.
Though I watched regular TV shows and movies (my favorites were How I Met Your Mother, The Office, Avatar (the Nickelodeon one), and Lord of the Rings), I was most drawn to the concept of YouTubers. The idea of having a niche or personality so strong and unique that you've carved out your own portion of the internet that is all yours. All they needed to do was "be themselves" and thousands of people would flock to watch and comment.
Over the next couple of years, this sentiment is what drew me to making videos. I started out with the trends at the time. I posted myself singing in the car, trying on makeup, doing a no hands challenge, and more. At one point, I even produced a small documentary around an arcade game that I played a lot at the time called Dancerush Stardom: https://youtu.be/Zq_mP85esDI
None of these videos got much traction. But without realizing it, I was gathering the basic skills, like getting a nice DSLR camera and tripod, knowing how to talk on camera, and self filming, lighting, audio, editing skills, and more.
2. My TikTok beginnings
Then COVID hit, TikTok took off, humanity changed forever, and Jess had her first taste of going "viral."
I remember the moment quite vividly. It was Valentine's Day in Seattle and there was a snowstorm happening. I couldn't drive, and walking outside to get groceries would be very difficult.
I was feeling bored and decided to film a video for TikTok about my career in tech so far. At the time I was working at Microsoft and just 1-2 years out of college, so I was still navigating the ins and outs of corporate politics, the career ladder, and software engineering as a whole. I wanted to start making videos to document my learnings, and that's what I talked about in my video. A couple hours after posting, it had 5000 views. The next day, over 30k. It was exhilarating. I was hooked.
After that, I started playing the game of social media. More videos = more views, so I started with the goal of pumping out 2 videos a day. After work, I'd spend the rest of the day planning, filming, and editing my videos. Every morning I'd wake up and immediately open TikTok to see how many notifications I had. I went viral a couple more times over the year. It was addicting. People liked my content because many wanted to work at Microsoft and were looking for tips around the interview process and transparency around the pay. Others wanted to break into software engineering and wanting leetcoding tips. 
This opened up a lot of doors for me. I was connected to a community that was constantly giving me advice or feedback in the comments of my videos. I hopped on a couple of virtual coffee chats during that time with people wanting to chat. My LinkedIn absolutely blew up; I was getting so many requests to connect every day. I was invited onto a podcast with other tech content creators and spoke with Scott Hanselman (who's a big deal). My manager at Microsoft even asked me to make a series of TikToks for the team to help spread an org announcement (I forget specifically what it was).
In this whole process, I realized that I'm not really built to be a social media personality (at least in the stereotypical sense). I didn't like anybody knowing that I made TikToks (especially because being a TikTok creator within my circles came with such a cringey connotation that it felt embarrassing). I'm not a natural speaking on camera, and one thing I'll admit is I actually script out every single one of my videos (to this day) before filming. I'm also a big introvert and didn't actually enjoy the community aspect of social media. I'm not excited by the idea of meeting influencers within my circle at a conference and having to socialize with them. Maybe it's partly because I also am not completely sold on this idea of corporate America and tech life. At the end of the day, it's just a means for me to make a paycheck and allow me to live my life of playing sports and exploring nature, and I like to keep one foot out of the tech world to protect my peace.
3. Career switch and lull in identity
Around 2021, I hit a wall with Microsoft and with software engineering. I hated my job, and I wanted to leave. I started interviewing for other places, and eventually accepted an offer as a software engineer at DoorDash. Funny story: one of my interviewers actually told me after my interview was completed that he knew me from TikTok. I don't know how much it affected me getting the job, but I'm guessing it couldn't have hurt 😂
After working at DoorDash for less than a year, I started to hate the job again. It was around that point that I realized software engineering wasn't for me. In a stroke of luck, one of my old friends was working as chief of staff at a startup called Warp. We had reconnected about playing flag football, and I found out that there was a position open for "dev rel." I had never heard of the term before, but I asked to interview and immediately started to do a lot of research. Ironically, I had a lot of time because I was taking medical leave from work due to fracturing my tibia and fibula from (you guessed it!) a football-related injury. I interviewed, got the job, and ended up working at Warp for the next 4 years.
This time was a difficult one in terms of my presence on social media. I had left Microsoft and moved to a relatively unknown startup. And I had left engineering to pivot into a relatively unknown role in tech (compared to roles like PM or sales). I didn't really know what videos to make anymore, and it felt like I wasn't getting the views that I had in the past. I stopped making videos on my personal channel for a little while.
4. Making content in a corporate role
Over my 4 years at Warp, I learned how to make content in a corporate role vs. for a personal channel. There were many different eras of this learning process, as the company/product grew and I myself matured into my devrel role.
My favorite was an era where I got to hire 2 folks I knew within the tech/content creation industry as contractors, and we worked as a team to produce content for Warp's YouTube channel. We grew it from <1k to >100k subscribers. I learned things like how to create viral hooks, how to work with a non-technical video editor, how to create a never-ending pipeline of content, and how to manage a content team across writing, filming, editing, and posting. That was one of the most fun times I've had in my career. I'm very grateful for those 2 folks @cpenned and @LewisMenelaws  (they are wonderful people and I love them very much ❤️).
In my job at Warp, I learned things like how to create product update videos, sizzle reels, and grow a YouTube channel, write technical articles, run technical workshops, talk at conferences, live on Twitter to understand trends, make documentary series, create "edutainment" style videos, use the power of editing for different forms of storytelling, do SEO/AEO, and understand how devrel plays into the marketing/product/engineering/sales functions.
Some of the things I struggled with were:
a)  The initial expectation of being the "face" of the company to represent at bigger conferences or to executive audiences, which I didn't feel confident with (maybe tied to my insecurities of not being technical enough or being a woman in a male-dominated space).
b) I also mentioned earlier in the article that I wasn't really good at turning my internet connections into networking/in-person opportunities, though that was the expectation of devrel (to be popular, well-known, well connected, and invited to a lot of exclusive things).
c) As the only devrel at the company for all 4 years, I got lost in the corporate sauce of it all. I often operated alone, and didn't have a good way to tie my impact back into a company North Star or collaborate with other functions of the company.
d) Pumping out content for the sake of content. It's probably why I'm not really very good at LinkedIn or X (no offense to any influencers on here). 
I could write a whole other article about these things, but I'll skip that for now. But moral of the story is I'm grateful for all these lessons I've learned. Though there are a lot of struggles, I love my career in devrel (definitely more than I would a traditional career in software engineering). My personal content on TikTok that I produced in 2020-2021 really opened that opportunity for me, and I learned a whole world of things outside of my TikTok days in the following 4-5 years of devrel.
5. Resurgence of personal content
In 2025, after a 3 year hiatus from my personal social media, I decided to start it back up again. This was around the time that AI started to take off, and the startup that I was working at was pivoting into the AI space. All of a sudden I was expected to know about MCP servers, skills, Claude Code, AI tokens, context windows, and more. I wanted a place to share my learnings, as well as a way to motivate myself to learn in a consistent way. Making content has always been a great way for me to do that. My goal when restarting was to hit 10k subscribers on YouTube. After building up momentum and starting to post ~3 times a week, I knew that goal would be achievable by the end of the year (And I'm currently sitting at 12k on Youtube, yay me!).
Posting content again opened up a lot of doors for me, yet again. I was getting brand deals from a plethora of AI companies. To this day, it still blows my mind how much money companies are willing to pay for a 60-second video. On top of that, I got some consulting opportunities that were a completely new experience for me. Funnily enough, at the time I had been wanting to switch companies for a while but didn't have much time/energy to recruit, and the company I ended up moving to next found me through my YouTube shorts. Honestly, content is so goated sometimes 😂
6. Reflections / what now?
1. I think that some people are meant to become immortal legends on social media, but most of us will have our era of popularity and then die out into obscurity. I suspect I'm experiencing "success" on social media right now because I'm very much ingrained in the AI space, which is a topic that is popular right now. But to be numbers feels like a vanity metric, and one day I'll fade into the background, just to be a very small blip in the large history of the internet.
2. Society's relationship with social media has changed so much since I've started. Nowadays, being an influencer or content creator is so much more commonplace. It feels less weird to make content and slowly I'm becoming more open about it and less embarrassed. And more and more I'm finding "role models" or influencer archetypes that really inspire me and I relate to.
3. I'm still figuring out my relationship between content -> business/moneymaking ventures. I will say, 2025 was the year that I learned the depths of influencer marketing at tech companies. At work, I sourced and hired influencers to work with and negotiated brand deals, and on the flip side I was also accepting brand deals for my own personal content. I became pretty worn out with the entire concept after a while. It felt like there were so many AI slop companies that were horrible products. I did take a couple of brand deals I regretted. For one of them, after trying out the product, I ended up refunding the money because I didn't want to promote it. On the other end of it, I was burnt out dealing with certain influencers (who were… in my very humble opinion… not fun to work with). My relationship with content and money is still up in the air. The idea of being able to make a full time income from content is nice, but I don't want to promote products I don't like or have to worry about the algorithm for my livelihood.
4. I like being super private on social media. There are things I see on social media of people posting about what they are doing on a certain holiday, or when they get married, and it's almost tempting to want to share more of my life because it's "easy" content, but it always makes me slightly uncomfortable and knowing where my limits lie is a really important lesson that I'm still exploring.

At the end of the day, I love making content. It pushes me to continue learning and teaching and makes me a better person. I still feel so inexperienced in many ways around setup/editing/speaking on camera, as well as trying to figure out how I want to "exist" on the internet that is authentically me and that I'm comfortable with, but also aligns with a successful a corporate/professional career. I'm excited to just keep chugging along and  see where the journey takes me 🙂

entries.listed_title: AI Slop, a generational marketing op, and the emerging trust crisis

article.plain_text:
LLMs are the ultimate producers. Point an LLM like Claude or Codex at a code feature, and it will quickly produce more code than you can imagine (or review). Ask Claude or Codex to write a blog post, and it will produce more words than you can imagine (or edit). An unshackled LLM is, in reality, a slop machine. It lives to produce more, and more, and more.
What is AI Slop? It is the love-child of the hasty, unconstrained, and unmoored LLM. It is noise over signal. It is verbose output as proof of work or knowledge. It is a quantity-based solution to a previously unimportant and ignored non-event. It is the diarrheal whimsy of recursive bot flirting. It's an unnecessary salutation, an extra intro paragraph for every conversation, an extra 20 words for every paragraph. It's the unnecessary transition between the last thought and the next. It's an app filled with shadowy code that seems to be working. It's a container arbitrarily filled to its absurd capacity.
AI Slop is a sharp violation of the pre-existing social, intellectual, and cultural principles we should hold dear.
AI Slop is the opposite of curated, constrained, and focused output. Slop brain is “hood rich.” More is better. More words with transitions == smarter. More code in 12 hours == better. More multitasking, more threads, more agents, and more loops == more productive.
AI Slop is infecting everything. We're seeing the first forerunners of a tsunami of content posts, content length, SaaS apps, emails, job posts, and all variety of fishy-smelling digital transmissions. AI Slop is antiquing concise and authentic content posts on places like X, Reddit, LinkedIn, and Instagram.
Previously impossible posts from unimportant, average, and unproductive people are now being loaded into the LLM turret gun. X accounts that rarely posted have gone from two-line replies to 2,000 word formulaic exposés on the curious relationship between previously unrelated entities. Tech voyeurs who can't write code for shit are now exhaustive experts on the latest AI and architecture trends. "Serial entrepreneurs" are launching new whimsical SaaS apps faster than we can properly pronounce Klaviyo. TikTokers have CapCut with AI scripts and transitions. Your #1 party bro from college just penned a 3,000 word Homeric epic for his son's 6th birthday, without a single typo or grammatical error. C'mon.
AI Slop is causing a huge loss of trust in the communication, network, and productivity channels we once relied on. It's getting so bad that content platforms and SaaS markets may never fully recover. We're moving toward a world where filtering through the noise looks like an impossible task. We've seen more norms change in the last 6 months than we have in 15 years.
I posted on X that Claude has been one of the greatest marketing ops of our lifetime. What I meant is that the LLMs fill a space in our psyche that we are so eager to fill. The marketing messaging and pro bono evangelism are recklessly helping us fill that void. The way that people flippantly adore and evangelize the capabilities of the latest model is getting a bit absurd and embarrassing. Every single expert I know who isn’t selling something says the same thing: each new model is a tragically mixed bag.
As with most things in life, multiple things can be true at once. Almost nothing is pure. We must take the good with the bad. The AI world is filled with competing quandaries. In some cases I'm very critical of AI, in others I'm a big evangelist. AI is incredible, annoying, earth-shattering, disappointing, and revolutionary, all at the same time. However we feel about AI and LLMs, the genie is never going back in the bottle. Everything is different now. We must accept our new future, warts and all.
With the massive inertia behind LLMs, it's difficult to pinpoint exactly where we are, and where we're going. If we pick a single point of analysis, like say, bug fixing, code review, or porting an existing app to another codebase or framework, we can probably claim those tasks are 100x better with LLMs.
It may be simply be that focus dictates quality. Is it Slop, or is it good, meaningful output? If an article gets more "impressions" does that mean the LLM made your writing better? Is content totally fake and inauthentic, or is it helpfully expanded by the LLM, providing color and scope to a unique vector of thought and process excellence. If our LLM code seems to work and has an automated test suite, but we don't read the code, how can we really know its quality?
My problem today as I evaluate all of my AI-enabled channels (code, SaaS, content, social, advertising), is that I just don't know. I don't trust content authors or social posts. I don't trust the code that LLMs write, and I don't have time to review the aggregate output. I don't trust my media spend and analytics. I'm not sure I trust anything transmitted, built, or marketed anymore. I don't know up from down. I can no longer easily identify quality, taste, and tradesmanship against mimicry slop. The environment is so polluted with low-vibration noise that it feels like I'm living inside of a massive marketing operation. It's unnerving.
This is my emerging crisis of trust.
Is our future one where we just turn off our brain and everything is on full-self-driving mode? Is our future one where everything is juvenile whimsy and the hard toiling of an author or builder is removed? Is our future one where we just agree that the LLMs are genius actors because we're lazy and don't want to suffer the grind to mastery? Is our future one of mere orchestration management, like a frazzled project manager who doesn't know enough to audit details that can lead to death? Will we just offload everything to an LLM and "phone it in" for 20 years in our career and our already fake social media life?
We've opened the gates to hell, and we'll have to find a way to a new future where the AIs remain subordinate to a dedicated human touch of passion, trade, and opinionated excellence. Part of modernity's promise was a flowering of the spirited and captivated individual. If we're not careful, our intellect will die to serve the robots through a THC-filled vape cloud of non-think, pajamas in public, and inauthentic vibes.
The direction of things seems a bit suspect. Some of the world's greatest minds are sounding the alarm. We may lose our wayfinding abilities, and our way of life.

entries.listed_title: Nothing Feels Real Anymore

article.plain_text:
I've noticed a trend. A lot of people give a lot of unsolicited advice about writing. But when you look at their body of work — other than this tendency to want to guide others they have no business guiding — their content falls flat. And yes, I just referred to it as content, because that’s exactly what it is. So what separates a legitimate piece of writing from mere content? Simply, it’s longevity. Will it apply well to somebody out of the context in which it was written? And is it timeless? More often than not, the answer is no. Now I’m not saying I don’t create content, I’m saying I aim higher.
Whether or not I fall short of that is for the reader to decide. Despite our best intentions as creatives nowadays, we need to come to the realisation that almost everything we post online is content. If an artist slaves away on a painting, and the end result is a photo of it uploaded to social media, it immediately becomes content. There’s something about experiencing art in person that heightens the stakes. It is then that it is seen in the context of a gallery co-signing its legitimacy. And if you think about it deeper — while this is a peculiar premise — it is most certainly a sound observation.
Now, I hate coming off like a snob, but I’ll take my lumps. The internet has cheapened what could potentially be great art, and turned it into something you scroll past. Unlike in person, when you’re confronted with the actual object itself. In this way, replicas have come to replace what is authentic. Nobody values digital media the same way they do physical media. Digital media feels impermanent, and almost throwaway. While physical media engages a large majority of ones senses. You can touch vinyl, sniff it, and hold it. The same goes for books. And this, to me, is important.
For those who’ve grown up in a world where the absence of physical media is rife, it makes me wonder how they process what they consume. The excitement can’t be there when receiving a digital download. And I say this all while publishing e-books. I know nothing can replace the genuine article. I’ll just come out and say it, the books I’m most proud of publishing have all gone to print. So where does this leave the future of art, literature and music? If everything is becoming digital, we don’t truly own anything, really. Therefore it’s a case of easy come, easy go. Is there hope?
We’ve seen a slight resurgence of physical media making a comeback, but it’s often treated as some kind of special, rare, merch bundle that is limited to only so many copies. This has to be because it involves risk, compared to posting something online, which can hardly be considered real publishing. Let’s face it, the large majority of people don’t buy physical media anymore. They just don’t. Only collectors and purists do. However, it’s not all gloom and doom. Now more than ever before it’s easier to get ones work out there with the help of the internet. And this creates opportunities.
But to what end? You can get your stuff out into the public, but actually having them reach into their pocket and buy what you’re selling is a whole different conversation. This is where the drawback comes in. What good is blowing up, getting attention, or being famous when you’re still broke? There is no upside. You’re now seen as something of a success with no earnings to show for it. In this way, the internet is actually killing the arts, all round. I understand why people don’t buy e-books. But it’s the low overhead that’s appealing, and the ease of publishing that’s the draw, for those who write them.
A lot of what I’m saying is unpleasant to consider, and I won’t deny that. But it’s the reality of the world we live in today. So there’s two things going on here. One, people don’t value digital media the way they do physical media. And two, physical media is being overlooked in service to digital. It’s a peculiar state of affairs to say the very least, but one phrase comes to mind immediately: you’ll own nothing, and you’ll be happy. Think about the subscriptions any given person has today — they’re all for digital services. If you go through your bank statement and add it up, you might be shocked.
Then again, it’s the new normal. We’re chronically online. We think we need these things, and therefore we pay through the nose for them. It’s only when we’re down to the wire at the end of the month that we even consider cancelling a subscription of some sort. Because most people are living pay cheque to pay cheque (in case you haven’t noticed). But no amount of bellyaching about the way things are will make them change. It’s just interesting to analyse human behaviour in this instance. How did we get to a place where we paid for things we can’t even touch or feel? We’ve been duped.
I remember getting a CD for Christmas one year, and opening up the booklet inside almost immediately. I wanted to read the lyrics in there. I didn’t go to Genius to look them up like I do now. And when an artist I adore releases a new album today, I go straight to YouTube to listen to it. But the excitement isn’t there. It’s just not the same. And the state of music is appalling now, anyway. Nothing sounds original, and the basest negativity of the human race is celebrated in it all. And while I get that sex sells, I don’t think music videos should amount to soft porn. Damn, I’m getting old.
Whatever it is the music industry did to Hip Hop is shameful, too. In a genre where real lyricism and technical ability were once championed, what we hear now is a far cry from all that. The issue is that Hip Hop infiltrated the Pop charts, and they became one and the same, with the commercialisation of an art form that had its roots in subverting mainstream tastes. The whole endeavour is very cannibalistic and off-putting, but I’ll just continue to reminisce on the good old days and try ignore the dreck that’s being served up today. I grew up in the golden age of Hip Hop.
Another thing that has long since disappeared off the charts is Rock in any form whatsoever. And that’s an indication of where the head space of kids today lies. They’re not interested in fuddy duddy stuff like guitars and real instruments. But playing those very instruments is what used to make someone a musician. I’m in no way trying to infer that Rock needs to make a comeback or anything of the sort, but I know I grew up on some Rock, and it moved me in ways other genres didn’t. If you had to press me on my tastes, I’d probably err on the side of the experimental acts and not pure Rock.
Collecting books by authors I love is something I enjoy doing. While I could buy audiobooks or Kindle editions, I value having a physical library. I want to be able to interact with the object itself, turn the pages — but one thing I won’t do is mark books up. I’ve never understood that practice. It seems sacrilegious to me. And I get that people like taking notes — and it speaks to interacting with the work, like I said — but can’t you use a notebook alongside the actual book instead? I guess this speaks to my involvement in the comic book industry, where “mint condition” is a very real thing.
All throughout history we wrote physical letters, bought records and collected books. To have that all go away in service to the digitisation of media, to me, is incredibly sad. But the internet is the go-to place these days, and so it’s only natural that it’d override all those other activities. Receiving an email doesn’t feel the same as getting something in the post. It’s reduced to yet another annoying notification. The biggest scam that was ever pulled on us was renting out space in the cloud. I don’t mean to rain on anyone’s parade, but it irritates me to no end. I just want to log off for a couple hours.
Every morning when I get up, I dread turning on my cell phone. I have no idea what new notifications, texts or calls I’ll get. It creates a type of uneasy anticipation and anxiety. If I could live without a phone I would. Being able to be contacted is the bane of my existence. Not even a dumb phone allows you to escape ones dreaded ring tone, whatever it may be. Perhaps I need to go live on a remote island somewhere, and disappear. Maybe then I’ll feel less tethered to the constant interruptions associated with modern living. Fortunately, most days I come away unscathed. But it’s the looming unknown that destroys an semblance of peace.

article.title: Nothing Feels Real Anymore

entries.listed_title: The Space Between Notifications: Why Stillness Matters More Than Ever

article.plain_text:
There was a time when waiting was simply part of everyday life.
People waited in line at the grocery store. They sat quietly in the waiting rooms. They looked out the window during a train ride. They stood in elevators without feeling the need to fill every second with activity. Those moments weren't always exciting, but they gave their minds something people rarely think about today.
Space.
Not empty space.
Thinking space.
The kind of space where ideas began to form, conversations replayed in people's minds, problems slowly found solutions, and emotions had an opportunity to settle before they reacted to them.
Today, those moments often look very different.
The instant people find themselves waiting, many instinctively reach for their phones. A few minutes at a coffee shop become an opportunity to check email. Standing in line becomes another chance to scroll through social media. A quiet evening on the couch quickly fills with notifications, short videos, headlines, and messages.
None of these moments seem particularly significant.
The question is what happens when they disappear altogether.
Over the past several years, Lora Lynn Bridges has spent countless hours speaking with students, educators, parents, and professionals about digital wellness. While every audience brings a different perspective, one observation continues to surface.
Many people no longer feel comfortable with stillness.
Not because they dislike peace or quiet, but because they have become accustomed to constant input. When there isn't a notification to answer or something new to consume, many people experience an almost immediate urge to fill the silence.
That response isn't a personal weakness.
It reflects the environment people have gradually adapted to.
Modern technology has given immediate access to information, entertainment, communication, and opportunity. Those advances have transformed the way people live, learn, and work. They have also reduced the number of moments in the day when minds are simply allowed to wander.
For generations, boredom was considered an inconvenience.
Today, Lora Lynn Bridges wonders if it might actually be a gift.
Some of our best ideas arrive when we're not actively searching for them. Creativity often appears during a walk, while washing dishes, driving home, or sitting quietly without a screen competing for our attention. Reflection works much the same way. It rarely appears in the middle of constant activity. It grows in the spaces between.
Lora Lynn Bridges has watched this happen in classrooms.
Article content
 
The students who are given a moment to pause before answering often share deeper thoughts than those who respond immediately. Lora Lynn Bridges has seen it in conversations with families as well. Some of the most meaningful discussions begin after a few moments of quiet, when people stop reacting and start listening
Stillness gives people room to notice.
They notice how they're feeling.
They notice what has been occupying their minds.
They notice the people sitting across from them.
Most importantly, they notice themselves.
That awareness is becoming increasingly valuable.
Every notification asks for our attention. Every recommendation invites another click. Every alert suggests that something deserves our immediate response. While many of these interactions are useful, they also make uninterrupted moments increasingly rare.
This isn't a reason to reject technology.
It's a reminder to protect the moments technology cannot create for us.
No application can manufacture genuine presence.
No algorithm can replace quiet reflection.
No notification can offer the same clarity that sometimes arrives after sitting with our thoughts for a few uninterrupted minutes.
This is one of the reasons Lora Lynn Bridges often encourages people to notice what happens before they unlock their phones.
Not after.
Before.
That brief pause creates an opportunity to ask a different question.
"Do I need my phone right now, or do I simply need a moment?"
 
Sometimes the answer will be the phone.
Sometimes it will be the moment.
Learning to recognize the difference is an important part of digital wellness.
It's also part of what SMARTlove™ represents.
SMARTlove™ has never been about measuring screen time or counting notifications. It is about building a relationship with technology that reflects our values rather than our impulses. When people become intentional about how they use their devices, they create more room for the conversations, creativity, and relationships that deserve their full attention.
Lora Lynn Bridges has often said that awareness creates choice.
Stillness helps create awareness.
Without moments to pause, it becomes much more difficult to notice where our attention is going or why we're reaching for our phones in the first place.
Perhaps that is why stillness matters more today than ever before.
Not because the world has become louder.
But because quiet has become more difficult to find.
The good news is that people don't have to wait for life to slow down before experiencing it.
People can choose to leave the phone in their pocket during a short walk.
They can allow a few minutes of silence before turning on a podcast during the drive home.
They can enjoy a meal without placing a screen between themselves and the people around them.
These aren't dramatic changes.
They are small choices that create space for something people have always needed.
Attention.
Presence.
Reflection.
The space between notifications may seem insignificant. Yet it is often where our clearest thinking, our strongest relationships, and our deepest sense of peace quietly begin.
That conversation is one worth continuing.
Until next time,
Featuring Lora Lynn Bridges
Human Connection Expert
Digital Wellness Speaker
Creator of CELLVIRA™ & SMARTlove™
Connect with Lora Lynn Bridges
Digital wellness isn't simply about using technology wisely. It's about protecting the moments that help people think clearly, connect deeply, and live intentionally.
Whether you're an educator, parent, organizational leader, healthcare professional, or event planner, Lora Lynn Bridges partners with schools, businesses, and communities to explore healthier relationships with technology through keynote presentations, workshops, educator training, parent engagement sessions, and meaningful digital wellness conversations.
Learn more, book a speaking engagement, or explore additional resources at: www.digitaladdictionspeaker.com
